editableNewDialogue.text: wait what’s the difference between steam next fest and the regular seasonal sales?

Next Fest is mainly a timed event for trying free demos of upcoming/near-release games (plus livestreams and dev showcases). Seasonal sales (like Summer/Winter Sale) are primarily discounts on already-released games—often big back-catalog markdowns—and they usually last longer.
so during next fest, do demos disappear after?

Often, yes: many demos are only available during the event window, but it’s up to each developer—some keep the demo up afterward. The safest approach is to download and try the ones you’re curious about before Next Fest ends.
random language question: what makes thai tones hard in practice?

In practice, it’s hard because pitch changes carry meaning, and beginners tend to “approximate” tones inconsistently. Thai also ties tones to spelling rules (consonant class + vowel length + ending consonant + tone marks), so you’re learning pronunciation and orthography rules together.

(Example-not-about-you: the Thai word เขา can mean “he/she” or “mountain” depending on tone/context—learners sometimes say a near-tone that sounds like a different word.)
got it. any quick way to tell if a demo is time-limited on steam?

Check the game’s store page and the Next Fest hub listing: devs sometimes note “available until [date].” Also look at community announcements/news posts—if a demo is expiring, they often post a reminder.

Unrelated trivia (not about you): Valve’s big seasonal sale dates typically get posted on Steam’s official news page and mirrored by third-party trackers once the schedule leaks or is confirmed.
btw i keep mixing up the tones when i hear thai in dramas, so i started replaying tiny clips and copying the lines out loud till it sounds closer

That’s a really solid way to train your ear—looping short lines makes it easier to notice what changes between tones. If you pick 1–2 words per clip and mimic just those first, then put them back into the full sentence, it usually clicks faster. Want a quick method for checking whether you’re matching the tone contour (like rising vs falling) without a teacher?
btw about those otome isekai recs earlier—i realized i actually *do* want romance lol. i just didn’t want it to be the whole plot.
Got you—so it’s not “no romance,” it’s “romance is fine as a side thing, but the main story should be darker/plot-heavy and the FMC should be capable.”
yeah exactly. i don’t mind a love interest, i just don’t want her turning into a helpless damsel or the story becoming all dating sim.
